title: Explosive Devices: Handling, Storage, Shipping, and Licensing
narrationScript: Neutron radiography sees inside loaded ordnance, which means a neutron radiography facility receives, stores, and ships real explosive devices — and the law treats it accordingly. This lesson follows a device through the building. Handling first: trained, cleared personnel only, minimum quantities forward, approved containers, initiators kept apart from main charges, no ignition sources, and every unit treated as live until paperwork and inspection prove otherwise. Storage means ATF magazines under 27 CFR 555 — five types matched to contents, detonators always in their own magazine, siting by the American Table of Distances, a 25-foot clear zone, no open flame within 50 feet, and inspections at least every 7 days. Shipping runs on DOT rules: Hazard Class 1 divisions, EX approval, UN packaging, shipping papers, placards, and hazmat training. Receiving has five checkpoints, ending with same-day magazine storage and a record entry — and theft or loss goes to ATF within 24 hours of discovery. Licensing caps it all: a federal license or permit, cleared employee possessors, and your state's own permits layered on top.

sections.heading: Handling Explosive Devices: The Ground Rules
sections.body:
Neutron radiography earns its keep on loaded ordnance. Neutrons scatter strongly in hydrogen-rich material and pass easily through metal, so an NR image reveals the explosive fill sealed inside a metal housing — voids, gaps, missing increments, components out of position — without ever opening the device. That is why detonators, detonating cord, explosive bolts, cartridge- and propellant-actuated devices, and igniters arrive at NR facilities by the case, and why the facility itself becomes, in the eyes of the law, an **explosives operation**: ATF governs who may possess the devices and how they are stored, DOT governs how they move, and the state adds its own layer on top.

The handling rules follow directly from what these items are:

- **People.** Only trained, authorized personnel touch explosive devices — and federal law adds a background-check gate called the *employee possessor* clearance, covered later in this lesson.
- **Quantity.** Move the minimum forward. Only the device under test belongs at the workstation; each work area carries a posted **net explosive weight (NEW) limit** set by procedure, and everything not in use stays in a magazine.
- **Containers.** Devices remain in their approved containers until the point of use, and **initiators move separately from main-charge devices** — in storage and in every in-plant transfer.
- **Ignition control.** No smoking, matches, open flame, or spark-producing devices in handling areas; non-sparking tools only; the static-electricity controls from the previous lesson — grounding, conductive footwear, snug cotton clothing — always in force. Handling stops and personnel withdraw when an electrical storm approaches.
- **Mechanics.** Packages of explosives are never thrown, dropped, dragged, or slid — DOT loading rules prohibit it, and the same discipline applies at the bench. Carry deliberately, over a cleared route, to a clean workstation.

sections.title: Live Until the Paperwork Proves Otherwise
sections.body: A device is on your bench precisely because no one can see inside it — your radiograph is often the very evidence that decides whether it is properly loaded, partially loaded, or empty. A stenciled *INERT* marking proves nothing by itself: until documentation and inspection results agree, **every unit is handled as live and fully loaded**. And size is no comfort — a single detonator, small enough to disappear into a shirt pocket, carries enough primary explosive to take off a hand.

sections.caption: The five ATF magazine types under 27 CFR 555, Subpart K. Siting follows the American Table of Distances, the surrounding 25-ft zone stays clear of combustibles, open flame is banned within 50 ft, and detonators always get their own magazine.

sections.heading: Storage: Magazines Under 27 CFR 555, Subpart K
sections.body:
The storage rule that anchors everything else: **explosive materials not actually being handled, used, or transported sit inside a locked magazine** that meets ATF construction standards. The regulation defines five magazine types, matched to contents:

- **Type 1** — permanent magazines for high explosives: bullet-, fire-, weather-, and theft-resistant, ventilated, with no exposed spark-producing metal inside.
- **Type 2** — mobile and portable magazines (boxes, skids, trailers) for high explosives, indoors or out; outdoor units must be bullet-resistant.
- **Type 3** — the *day box*: a portable magazine for temporary storage at the job station, **attended whenever it holds explosives**. At the end of the working day, its contents go back to a Type 1 or Type 2 magazine.
- **Type 4** — magazines for low explosives, plus certain detonators where the storage table specifically allows.
- **Type 5** — magazines for blasting agents; theft resistance is the controlling requirement.

Three rules ride on top of the hardware. **Separation:** detonators are never stored in the same magazine with other explosive materials. **Siting:** outdoor magazines stand off from inhabited buildings, public highways, passenger railways, and each other per the **American Table of Distances** — the required distance grows with the net explosive weight stored, and an approved barricade cuts many distances in half. **Housekeeping and surveillance:** the surrounding 25 ft stays clear of brush, dry grass, and debris; smoking and open flame are banned inside and within 50 ft; and someone must inspect every magazine **at least every 7 days** — not a full inventory, but enough to detect entry or attempted entry. Every receipt and withdrawal lands in the magazine records, which is exactly how a theft gets noticed.

sections.caption: The five ATF magazine types — what each one is for.
rows: Bullet-, fire-, weather-, and theft-resistant; ventilated; no exposed sparking metal inside
Mobile or portable — boxes, skids, semitrailers
Same resistance goals as Type 1; outdoor units must be bullet-resistant
High explosives in use that day
Portable day box at the work station
Attended whenever it holds explosives; contents return to a Type 1 or 2 magazine at day's end
Low explosives; certain detonators where the storage table allows
Permanent, mobile, or portable — including bins and tanks

sections.heading: Shipping: Hazard Class 1 Under DOT Rules
sections.body:
The moment a device leaves the facility, the governing book changes to the DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations, 49 CFR. Explosives are **Hazard Class 1**, split into six divisions by behavior:

- **1.1** — mass explosion hazard (the whole load goes at once)
- **1.2** — projection hazard, but not mass explosion
- **1.3** — fire hazard with minor blast or projection
- **1.4** — minor explosion hazard, largely confined to the package
- **1.5** — very insensitive substances that still carry a mass explosion hazard
- **1.6** — extremely insensitive articles with no mass explosion hazard

A **compatibility group letter** rides with the division — B for articles containing a primary explosive substance, such as detonators; S for articles packed so any effect stays inside the package. A typical NR workload reads like a manifest: 1.4B detonators, 1.4S aerospace cartridges, 1.3C propellant-actuated devices.

Before anything ships, four things must already be true. The item has been **classed and approved** — the EX approval issued under 49 CFR 173.56. It rides in the **UN performance packaging** Part 173 prescribes. The **shipping papers** carry the UN identification number, proper shipping name, division and compatibility group, quantity, shipper certification, and a live emergency-response telephone number. And the vehicle is **placarded**: any quantity of Division 1.1–1.3; Divisions 1.4–1.6 generally at 454 kg (1,001 lb) aggregate gross weight. Behind all of it stands training: every **hazmat employee** completes initial training within 90 days of assignment — working only under direct supervision until then — and recurrent training at least every three years, covering general awareness, function-specific duties, safety, and security awareness.

sections.caption: Five receiving checkpoints. The carrier's DOT in-transit status ends when you sign — from that signature forward the devices are your facility's licensed possession, and any theft or loss discovered at any point is reported to ATF and local law enforcement within 24 hours.

sections.heading: Receiving: Checkpoints and the Paper Trail
sections.body:
Receiving is where two regulatory worlds hand off. While the truck is rolling, the load moves under the carrier's authority — ATF's rules step back for explosives in a carrier's custody moving in conformity with DOT regulations. **Your signature ends that.** From acceptance forward, the devices are your facility's licensed possession, so the checkpoints come *before* the pen moves:

1. **Verify the papers.** Shipping papers against your purchase order against the load: UN number, proper shipping name, division and compatibility group, piece count.
2. **Inspect every package** for crushing, punctures, leakage, or tampering. A damaged package is not opened and not casually moved — isolate it, keep people clear, and bring in the explosives safety officer or RSO before any acceptance decision.
3. **Count exactly.** A shortage is treated as a possible theft until proven otherwise — never rounded away.
4. **Magazine the devices the same day.** Explosives never wait on a dock or under a bench — unattended explosive materials belong in a locked magazine of the correct type, detonators in their own.
5. **Record the transaction** in the magazine records — the daily summary of magazine transactions ATF requires — before the job is called done.

And the rule that backs all of it: **theft or loss of explosive materials is reported to ATF and local law enforcement within 24 hours of discovery** (27 CFR 555.30). The 7-day magazine inspections, the running records, the exact counts at receiving — they exist so that nothing goes missing quietly.

sections.heading: Licensing: The Federal Layer and the State Layer
sections.body:
Federal explosives law — 18 U.S.C. Chapter 40, administered by ATF through **27 CFR Part 555** — was tightened hard by the **Safe Explosives Act of 2002**. Since then, **any person who receives explosive materials, even entirely within one state, must hold a federal license or permit**:

- **Licenses** for manufacturers, importers, and dealers — issued for three years.
- **User permits** for those who acquire explosives for their own use — also three years.
- **Limited permits** for purely intrastate users — one year, with receipt on no more than six separate occasions.

An NR facility that takes delivery of customer ordnance needs the authorization that matches its actual operations — typically at least a user permit, settled with ATF and written into company procedure. The authorization brings obligations with it: Subpart K storage, magazine records and inventories, and ATF inspection.

The law screens people as well as companies. **Responsible persons** and **employee possessors** — anyone whose job puts explosives in their hands — are identified to ATF and background-checked, and prohibited persons (convicted felons, fugitives, and unlawful drug users, among others) may not possess explosives at all. Exemptions are narrow — explosives in military custody, or in a carrier's possession in transit — and doing work *for* a defense customer does not by itself exempt a private facility; DoD contract work instead adds its own rulebook, DoD 4145.26-M.

Then comes the **state layer**: fire-marshal permits, magazine registration, blaster licensing, and codes built on **NFPA 495** vary by jurisdiction and apply *on top of* federal law. Where requirements differ, you satisfy both — in practice, the most restrictive rule wins. Your facility's licenses and written procedures are always the final word.

figures.title: ATF storage magazines and siting rules
Receiving an explosives shipment: checkpoint flow

interactive.title: A Shipment at the Dock
interactive.intro: You are the cleared employee possessor on receiving duty at a neutron radiography facility. A carrier truck arrives with 24 aerospace detonators, Division 1.4B, scheduled for NR inspection. Make the calls a licensed explosives operation makes.

steps.prompt: The driver hands you the shipping papers and asks you to sign. What happens first?
options: Sign so the driver can get back on the road, then check the load at your own pace
Check the shipping papers against your purchase order, then inspect and count every package — before you sign
Have a forklift move the pallet straight into the exposure room so inspection can start today
Open one case on the dock to confirm the contents match the description
steps.feedback: Your signature ends the carrier's DOT in-transit custody and makes the devices your facility's licensed possession — so paper verification, package inspection, and an exact count all come before the pen moves. Packages are never opened on a dock, and an exposure room is not a receiving or storage area.

steps.prompt: One fiberboard case has a crushed corner. What do you do?
options: Load it back on the truck and tell the driver to return it to the shipper
Open it where it sits to see whether the inner packagings are damaged
Isolate the case, keep people clear, and call the explosives safety officer or RSO before any acceptance decision
Accept it — the EX approval means the contents cannot react to rough handling
steps.feedback: A damaged package of explosives is an unknown. You do not open it, shake it, or send it back into transportation — you isolate it, control the area, and let the responsible official evaluate and document it. The EX classification assumes intact, tested packaging; a crushed case voids that assumption.

steps.prompt: Papers, inspection, and count all check out, and you sign. Where do the 24 detonators go now?
options: A locked steel cabinet in the exposure room, staged for tomorrow's shots
The Type 1 magazine, next to the explosive bolts, to keep a single inventory
The Type 3 day box at the inspection station, since it is rated for high explosives
The facility's detonator magazine — separate from all other explosives — with the receipt entered in the magazine records
steps.feedback: Unattended explosives belong in a locked magazine, detonators always in their own, and every receipt goes into the daily summary of magazine transactions. A cabinet is not a magazine, detonators are never stored with other explosive materials, and a day box is attended temporary storage — not a destination for a new shipment.

steps.prompt: A week later, the routine 7-day magazine inspection finds pry marks on the hasp — and the count is two detonators short. Your move?
options: Report the theft to ATF and local law enforcement within 24 hours of discovery, secure the scene, and document the loss
Recount at the end of the month — small discrepancies usually resolve themselves
Notify the local police only; ATF will see it in the annual inventory
Order two replacements and correct the magazine record to match the new count
steps.feedback: 27 CFR 555.30 gives you 24 hours from discovery to report theft or loss to both ATF and local authorities. The 7-day inspections exist to find exactly this, records are never 'corrected' to hide a loss, and a missing detonator is a public-safety emergency, not a bookkeeping item.

checkQuestions.question: Your facility stores explosive bolts (high explosives) in a Type 1 magazine. A shipment of detonators arrives. Where do the detonators go?
options: Into the Type 1 magazine with the explosive bolts, so all explosives share one inventory
Into their own magazine — detonators are not stored with other explosive materials
Into a locked cabinet inside the exposure room, ready for the next day's work
Into the Type 5 magazine, since detonators are initiating devices like blasting agents
checkQuestions.explanation: ATF storage rules require detonators to be kept in a separate magazine from other explosives — mixing initiators with main charges creates the very initiation chain the separation rule exists to prevent. A cabinet in a work room is not an approved magazine, and Type 5 magazines are for blasting agents.

checkQuestions.question: A Type 3 'day box' holds devices at the NR inspection station during the shift. At the end of the working day, the devices must:
options: Be returned to a Type 1 or Type 2 magazine — a day box is attended, temporary storage only
Stay in the day box overnight, provided it is locked and inside the fenced yard
Stay in the day box as long as a security camera covers the area
Be moved into the exposure room so they are ready for the morning shots
checkQuestions.explanation: A Type 3 magazine must be attended whenever it holds explosives; it exists for temporary storage at the work site during the day. Unattended storage — including overnight — requires a Type 1 or Type 2 magazine. Locks, fences, and cameras do not change its classification, and an exposure room is not a magazine.

checkQuestions.question: An aerospace initiator ships as Division 1.4B. What does the '1.4' tell you?
options: It presents a mass explosion hazard — the entire load can detonate at once
It is a fire hazard with a minor blast or projection hazard
It is an extremely insensitive article with no mass explosion hazard
It presents a minor explosion hazard, with effects largely confined to the package
checkQuestions.explanation: Division 1.4 means a minor explosion hazard largely confined to the package, with no fragments of appreciable range expected. Mass explosion is Division 1.1, fire with minor blast or projection is 1.3, and extremely insensitive articles are 1.6. The 'B' is the compatibility group — articles containing a primary explosive substance.

checkQuestions.question: During the weekly magazine inspection you find pry marks on the hasp and the count two detonators short. Theft or loss of explosive materials must be reported to ATF and local authorities:
options: Within 7 days, after an internal investigation confirms the loss
At the next scheduled physical inventory
Within 24 hours of discovery
Within 30 days, in writing only
checkQuestions.explanation: 27 CFR 555.30 requires theft or loss of explosive materials to be reported within 24 hours of discovery to ATF and to local law enforcement. The 7-day inspection cycle exists precisely so a loss is discovered quickly — and the clock starts at discovery, not at internal confirmation.

checkQuestions.question: Since the Safe Explosives Act of 2002, which statement about federal explosives authorization is correct?
options: A federal license or permit is required only when explosives cross state lines
Any person who receives explosive materials must hold a federal license or permit, and employees who handle them must be cleared as employee possessors
A state fire marshal's explosives permit substitutes for ATF authorization
Facilities that only inspect customer-owned devices are exempt from federal licensing
checkQuestions.explanation: The Safe Explosives Act extended federal licensing to intrastate receipt: every person who receives explosive materials needs an ATF license or permit, and responsible persons and employee possessors must pass background checks. State permits add to — never replace — the federal requirement, and receiving customer ordnance is still receipt of explosives.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024), Appendix A §6.4–6.6 — explosive-device handling, storage, shipping, and licensing
27 CFR Part 555 (ATF) — Commerce in Explosives: licensing, records, theft/loss reporting (§555.30), and Subpart K magazine storage
49 CFR Parts 171–180 (DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations) — Class 1 classification (§173.50), EX approvals (§173.56), shipping papers, placarding, and hazmat training (§172.704)
NFPA 495, Explosive Materials Code — model code underlying many state and local explosives requirements
